1: "No one who has been emasculated may enter the assembly of the LORD." This exclusion stands until Isaiah 56:3–7 explicitly reverses it: "let not the eunuch say, 'I am a dry tree'... I will give them... a name better than sons and daughters." Philip's baptism of the Ethiopian eunuch (Acts 8:26–39) is the fulfillment: someone legally excluded from Israel's assembly enters the community of Christ, becoming a full member. The new covenant opens what the old covenant restricted; Christ's work is the expansion of the assembly to all.
2: "No one born of a forbidden union may enter the assembly of the LORD — not even down to the tenth generation." The law's generation-spanning exclusion is the negative image of YHWH's covenant blessing to "a thousand generations" (Deuteronomy 5:10). In Christ, the generational curse is broken: "there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus" (Romans 8:1). No lineage of shame is permanent; the new creation in Christ overrides the genealogical restrictions of the old covenant community.
3: "No Ammonite or Moabite may enter the assembly of the LORD — never, for all time." The permanent exclusion of Moab stands in the law. Yet Ruth the Moabite, through Boaz's kinsman-redemption, enters the covenant community and becomes an ancestor of David and Christ (Matthew 1:5). The "never, for all time" of the law is not violated but superseded by the grace that was always operative within and beyond the law's boundary conditions.
4: Moab refused to provide bread and water to Israel in the desert and hired Balaam to curse them. The failure of hospitality and the act of active cursing — both are covenant violations. The hired curse becomes the background for one of the OT's clearest curse-to-blessing reversals, which Paul cites as the pattern of the cross (Galatians 3:13).
5: "But the LORD your God refused to listen to Balaam and turned the curse into a blessing for you, because the LORD your God loves you." This verse is the structural antecedent to Galatians 3:13–14: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us... so that in Christ Jesus the blessing of Abraham might come to the Gentiles." The cross is the definitive Balaam-reversal — the enemy's curse (the law's condemnation) turned to blessing (justification and adoption) not by blocking the curse but by Christ absorbing it and emerging in resurrection blessing.
6: "Never seek their friendship or wellbeing as long as you live." The ongoing hostility toward Moab and Ammon stands in the law. Christ's command to "love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you" (Matthew 5:44) is not a simple contradiction but a fulfillment of the love-commandment that transcends the specific political boundaries of the Mosaic covenant. The universality of the gospel is precisely this: no group is permanently excluded from the possibility of salvation (Colossians 1:20).
7: "Do not despise an Edomite — he is your brother. Do not despise an Egyptian — you lived as a foreigner in his land." The logic of memory and kinship moderating hostility: Israel remembers its own experience as sojourners and adjusts its attitude toward outsiders. Paul's argument in Ephesians 2:12 ("remember that at that time you were... foreigners to the covenants of promise") uses the same logic: those who were outsiders remember their former status and do not despise current outsiders.
8: "Their descendants in the third generation may enter the assembly of the LORD." The graduated inclusion of Edom and Egypt — kinship and memory create a path toward assembly membership. The new covenant removes the time-delay entirely: "anyone who comes to me I will never drive away" (John 6:37). The gradual inclusion of the old covenant becomes the immediate welcome of the new, secured by Christ's once-for-all act of inclusion (Ephesians 2:13–19).
9: "When you are camped against an enemy, keep yourself from every form of evil." The holiness requirement intensifies in the military camp. The principle behind camp purity applies to the church at war: "your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour" (1 Peter 5:8). Paul calls for the full armor of God (Ephesians 6:10–18) — spiritual camp discipline before the spiritual enemy — grounded in Christ's own victory that provides the armor.
10: Ceremonial impurity from a nocturnal emission — the warrior leaves the camp temporarily. The tension between sexual life and holiness in the military camp reflects the creation-gift of sexuality within the covenant community's discipline. Paul's application in 1 Corinthians 7:3–5 navigates the same tension: sexual union within marriage is a positive good; but seasons of prayer and fasting may call for temporary abstinence — the discipline of the camp, applied to the household of God.
11: "At evening he shall wash with water, and after sunset he may return to the camp." The washing and return — the purification rite restoring covenant standing. Baptism in the NT corresponds to this kind of washing: "you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God" (1 Corinthians 6:11). The camp's purification ritual is a type of the definitive cleansing that Christ's death and the Spirit's washing provide.
12: Designate an area outside the camp for waste. The practical sanitation law embedded in the covenant reveals YHWH's concern for the whole life of the community — bodily as well as spiritual. Christ's concern extends to the whole person: he feeds the hungry (Matthew 14:13–21), heals bodies (Matthew 8:1–17), and attends to practical need as well as spiritual. The Creator-God's law governs the bodies he made, and Christ restores them.
13: "Carry a small trowel... dig a hole and cover your waste when you are done." The attention to physical detail in the holiness code reflects that YHWH's covenant covers the whole of life, including the most mundane bodily functions. Paul extends covenant holiness into every bodily act: "whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God" (1 Corinthians 10:31). In Christ, no area of life is outside the scope of redemption and holiness.
14: "For the LORD your God moves throughout your camp to protect you." The divine presence in the camp requires camp purity. The same principle governs the church: "do you not know that you are God's temple and that God's Spirit dwells in you?" (1 Corinthians 3:16). Christ is present in the gathered community (Matthew 18:20: "where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them"). His presence both consecrates and demands holiness.
15: "Do not return a runaway slave to his master if he has sought refuge with you." The law of asylum for the escaped slave — the covenant community is a place of refuge for those fleeing bondage. Christ's community is the ultimate asylum: "come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest" (Matthew 11:28). The freedom he offers is not the freedom of the runaway slave (temporary, precarious) but the liberty of the adopted child (permanent, certain — Galatians 4:5–7).
16: "Let him live with you in whatever town he chooses... do not mistreat him." The runaway slave is integrated into the community and protected — not returned to bondage. Paul's letter to Philemon operates in the space between this principle and Roman law: he sends Onesimus back but urges Philemon to receive him "no longer as a slave, but as a dear brother" (Phlm 16). The new covenant community transforms the relationship between master and slave without violently overturning social structures — working from within toward the freedom Deuteronomy 23:15–16 already envisioned.
17: "No Israelite woman shall become a cult prostitute, and no Israelite man shall become a cult prostitute." The ban on religious prostitution protects sexuality from co-optation by Baal fertility cult. Paul's concern in 1 Corinthians 6:12–20 is the same: the body is "a temple of the Holy Spirit" (v19) and must not be joined to a prostitute (v15–16). Christ's redemption includes the body: "you were bought with a price. So glorify God in your body" (v20).
18: "Do not bring the earnings of a prostitute or the wages of a male prostitute into the house of the LORD your God." The principle of uncontaminated worship — the offerings must not be funded by what is abominable. The Pharisees' refusal to put Judas's thirty pieces of silver into the temple treasury (Matthew 27:6) applies this exact principle. The irony is deep: the money used to betray the Son of God was recognized as tainted — yet Christ's death purchased by that money is the one truly pure offering for sin.
19: "Do not charge a fellow Israelite interest on money, food, or anything else." The interest-free economy within the covenant community — grace-economics where brothers lend freely. Jesus's teaching extends this: "give to the one who asks you, and do not turn away from the one who wants to borrow from you" (Matthew 5:42); "lend, expecting nothing in return" (Luke 6:35). The covenant community's economics become the pattern for the kingdom community.
20: "You may charge a foreigner interest, but not your fellow Israelite." The differential reflects the boundary between covenant community and the surrounding world. In Christ, the boundary shifts: all believers are "fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God" (Ephesians 2:19), and the interest-free brotherhood principle now applies across ethnic lines. The family of God in Christ is the fulfillment of the "fellow Israelite" category — expanded to include all who are in Christ.
21: "When you make a vow to the LORD your God, do not delay in fulfilling it." The principle of prompt covenant faithfulness. Christ is "the same yesterday, today, and forever" (Hebrews 13:8) — the one whose promises never lag. He is "the Amen" (2 Corinthians 1:20) and "the Yes" to all of God's promises: "all the promises of God find their Yes in him." The standard for vow-keeping that Israel struggled to meet is perfectly embodied in Christ.
22: "But if you choose not to make a vow at all, you incur no guilt." The law of non-compulsion: vows are voluntary; not making one is not a sin. Jesus elevates this by eliminating the vow-category entirely: "let what you say be simply 'yes' or 'no'; anything more than this comes from evil" (Matthew 5:37). The category distinction between vow and ordinary speech collapses because ordinary speech should carry the weight of total truthfulness — and Christ, who is Truth, speaks this way in every utterance.
23: "Be careful to do what you have promised, for you have made a freewill vow to the LORD your God with your own mouth." Vows bind; the spoken word creates obligation. Christ's own words create the ultimate obligation: "heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away" (Matthew 24:35). His promises are the most binding words in existence — what he has spoken will come to pass, not because it is merely spoken, but because the one who speaks it is Truth itself (John 14:6).
24: "When you enter your neighbor's vineyard, you may eat your fill of grapes, but you may not take any away in a container." The gleaning-access law — the neighbor's produce is available to the passing traveler's immediate need, but not for commercial harvesting. Jesus's disciples pluck grain as they walk through fields (Matthew 12:1; Luke 6:1), invoking this very law when challenged by the Pharisees. Jesus's defense ("have you not read what David did when he was hungry?" and "the Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath") reveals he is greater than both the humanitarian law and the Sabbath law it was used to invoke.
25: "When you walk through a neighbor's standing grain, you may pluck heads of grain by hand, but do not put a sickle to the grain." This is the law the disciples acted on (Matthew 12:1–8; Mark 2:23–28; Luke 6:1–5). The Pharisees' challenge — that the plucking was Sabbath labor — and Jesus's multi-layered response (David's precedent, priestly temple work, "something greater than the temple is here") reveals the principle beneath the principle: the son of man is the Lord whose authority over the law derives from being its author and fulfillment.
